name: machine-signal
description: Logical signal abstraction on top of Pin. Simplifies active-low signal handling via invert=True. Signal.on() always means 'logically on' regardless of physical polarity.
differences_from_cpython: Does NOT exist in CPython. MicroPython-specific abstraction over Pin.

classes.name: Signal

constructor_params.name: pin
constructor_params.description: Pin object, GPIO number, or string identifier.

constructor_params.name: invert
constructor_params.description: If True, physical pin level is inverted (on() sets pin low, off() sets pin high). Default False.

methods.name: Signal.value
methods.description: Get or set logical signal value. 0 or 1. With invert=True, reads inverted physical level.

methods.name: Signal.on
methods.description: Set signal logically high. If invert=True, sets pin LOW.

methods.name: Signal.off
methods.description: Set signal logically low. If invert=True, sets pin HIGH.

classes.example:
from machine import Pin, Signal

# Active-low LED (LED on when GPIO is LOW)
led = Signal(2, invert=True)
led.on()    # GPIO2 = LOW, LED is ON
led.off()   # GPIO2 = HIGH, LED is OFF

# Read inverted button (active-low with pull-up)
btn = Signal(0, invert=True)  # GPIO0, pull-up, active-low
if btn.value():  # True when button pressed
    print('Button pressed!')

esp32_notes: Signal is a pure software abstraction — no hardware dependency.
Useful for active-low LEDs, buttons, relays, and enable pins.
Underlying pin is accessible via Signal.pin if needed.
Signal class is small and lightweight — negligible memory overhead.
